name: Byron Stripling
abstract: Byron Stripling is a jazz trumpeter who has been a member of the Count Basie Orchestra.

quotes.text: Yeah, a group playing together. I mean I think they can play the song but I don’t want to hear but Lester play on the solo, I don’t want to hear his solo recreated. But that’s my personal opinion. I think it’s good for people to practice that stuff, and I practiced Louis Armstrong solos, I practiced Roy Eldridge solos, Kenny Dorham. I’ve got 25 I’ve transcribed and I try to play through almost like etudes. I work on all that stuff, but I don’t really want to record it because that’s what they did. And the difficult thing is to try to find your own identity as a jazz musician. And that’s what I’m trying to work on now. It’s the most humbling thing that can happen for you is to try to find your own voice in this music. And I just don’t want, and I hope, I really enjoy the way I play in the Carnegie Hall Jazz Band. I like the way that we do it. We’ll play like standards and stuff but do new arrangements of them, and that’s really nice. You don’t have to recreate anybody’s solo or anything like that. But you know it’s funny you’ve got to realize when the bands came up, when Thad started his band, it was like not let’s recreate something, it was just like okay let’s get some guys together and let’s make some great music. It’s hard with things, because then we are going the way of like the orchestras and that kind of stuff, when you get groups like Carnegie Hall, Lincoln Center. They are very valuable but I © Fillius Jazz Archive -20-
quotes.caption: Speaker discusses playing in the Carnegie Hall Jazz Band generally
quotes.doc_title: Byron Stripling interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, April 25, 1998 [interview 1 of 2]

quotes.text: hope that some other bands come along, you know Maria Schneider has a great band, and that’s the kind, that’s a great example. And Carnegie Hall and Lincoln Center do too. There’s something to be said for that struggle you have to go through as an artist. You know I’ve been with guys in Europe and terrible players sometimes and they’ll go, “Well you know the government pays me, I got a grant to do a tour with my jazz band” and this and that, “and they gave me $100,000 for the year.” And I’m just like man but you suck. I think there might be something to, you know when a guy goes to New York and there’s not much stuff in the — what do you call it — there’s no gigs for him, and he’s just sitting in that greasy apartment. He says “man, I’ve got to be the best, because there ain’t no work out here. I’ve got to get my stuff together.” And that’s what New York and places like that do for you. You’ve got to learn how to play then.
quotes.caption: General reflection on New York's artistic struggle
quotes.doc_title: Byron Stripling interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, April 25, 1998 [interview 1 of 2]

quotes.text: Oh no. It’s a great question. First of all actually what I had to discuss with my father was even better than what you described. He would say, “I know you were at Carnegie Hall last night” and I appreciated it. He and my mother. And I know that you played on a record date and you know, I was so lucky when I lived in New York, I was doing so much. What I would say to kids about that part of your life to and explaining it to your parents is, those challenges are good. It’s good to be able to defend what you’re doing. When I told my mother, I’ll never forget it, we lived on Wood Creek Court I think it was, in Texas. And I said, “Mom, I’m going to move to New York.” And she started crying. She was like, “Why don’t you go back and get your degree?” And this and that. And I had to explain what my vision was for that. And it’s good that I had to do that. You need to be able to tell people what you’re going to do and your strategy. For you to be able to verbalize that not only helps them understand you but it’s for you. You need to outline what path you’re going to take. Sometimes you don’t know that path and you’re on faith and you can say that too. Say I don’t know what it is but in my gut I know I need to be around the musicians that I’ve adored all my life. And sometimes that’s enough but if you can add to it you can. I’ll give you a quick story of somebody else. Do you know Dick Oatts, the great sax player? He’s a great alto player. Yeah. So Dick said he worked with Thad Jones at University of Minnesota. That’s where I first met Thad Jones, I lived in Minnesota. And Thad loved him. He said, “Man you sound great.” He says, “Look if you ever move to New York give me a call.” He was like wow, cool. So he flew into LaGuardia Airport about a year later and there was a pay phone there at LaGuardia Airport and he goes, “Hey Thad, I’m here.” But now here’s where it gets crazy. Thad goes, “Oh man, I remember you, Minnesota, alto player.” He said, “What are you doing tonight?” And Dick said, “Nothing.” He says, “We need a second alto” or something. This is like when he lands in LaGuardia from Minnesota. You know? So he went and I said, “How was that first night?” Here’s the funny part of the story. He goes, well — I don’t know if you ever saw the Thad Jones-Mel Lewis band but it was an amazing band that was totally spontaneous. It was also like a party many times, what they did on the bandstand. It was good times and just fun. And you never knew what Thad was going to do. I remember myself seeing that band a lot. He’d just cut off the whole band and Janice Robinson would just play trombone with her plunger, and then Thad would join her and they would talk with their plungers you know. And it was like you knew that they were swearing and arguing and telling jokes with their instruments. So anyway Dick was like I wonder if he’s going to give me a solo. And at one point he went like this [gestures] stand up, and he gave me the mic and told me to sing some blues. But that’s Thad Jones and © Fillius Jazz Archive -6-
quotes.caption: Father mentions Byron played at Carnegie Hall
